DPmixGPD: Model specification and posterior computation

Source: vignettes/basic-01.Rmd
basic-01.Rmd

DPmixGPD is built around a practical modeling principle: the distributional structure that fits the bulk (where most observations live) is often a poor description of the tail (where rare extremes live). The package therefore models the bulk using a Dirichlet process mixture (DPM) and, optionally, models the upper tail using a generalized Pareto distribution (GPD), spliced in a way that yields a single proper density on the full support.

This vignette defines the full statistical model and the posterior computation used throughout the package. The workflow vignettes (unconditional, conditional, causal) use this specification but avoid restating it.

Notation

Let y1,…,yNy_1,\dots,y_N be observed outcomes. When covariates are present, let 𝐱i∈ℝp\boldsymbol{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^p denote the ii-th row of a design matrix XX. Mixture components are indexed by jj (not hh). The Dirichlet process concentration parameter is κ>0\kappa>0.

Write the bulk density and CDF as fDPM(⋅∣𝐱)f_{\mathrm{DPM}}(\cdot\mid\boldsymbol{x}) and FDPM(⋅∣𝐱)F_{\mathrm{DPM}}(\cdot\mid\boldsymbol{x}). Write the GPD density and CDF as fGPD(⋅∣u(𝐱),σ(𝐱),ξ)f_{\mathrm{GPD}}(\cdot\mid u(\boldsymbol{x}),\sigma(\boldsymbol{x}),\xi) and FGPD(⋅∣u(𝐱),σ(𝐱),ξ)F_{\mathrm{GPD}}(\cdot\mid u(\boldsymbol{x}),\sigma(\boldsymbol{x}),\xi).

Bulk model: Dirichlet process mixture

Hierarchical specification

The bulk model is a kernel mixture with a Dirichlet process prior on the mixing distribution (Ferguson 1973). In allocation form, yi∣zi,{Θj}∼k(yi∣Θzi,𝐱i),zi∣𝐰∼Categorical(w1,w2,…), y_i \mid z_i,\{\Theta_j\}\;\sim\;k\!\left(y_i\mid\Theta_{z_i},\boldsymbol{x}_i\right), \qquad z_i\mid\boldsymbol{w}\;\sim\;\mathrm{Categorical}(w_1,w_2,\dots), Θj∣H∼iidH,H∼DP(κ,H0), \Theta_j\mid H\;\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}\;H,\qquad H\;\sim\;\mathrm{DP}(\kappa,H_0), where k(⋅∣Θj,𝐱)k(\cdot\mid\Theta_j,\boldsymbol{x}) is the chosen kernel family (Normal, Gamma, Lognormal, Laplace, Inverse Gaussian, Amoroso, etc.), H0H_0 is a base measure for the kernel parameters, and κ\kappa controls the expected number of occupied clusters.

Marginally this implies an (infinite) mixture representation [ f_{}(y)

= _{j=1}^{} w_j,k!(y_j,). ]

Stick-breaking representation and truncation

DPmixGPD provides a stick-breaking backend (backend="sb") based on Sethuraman’s construction (Sethuraman 1994). The weights are wj=Vj∏ℓ<j(1−Vℓ),Vj∼iidBeta(1,κ),Θj∼iidH0. w_j = V_j\prod_{\ell<j}(1-V_\ell), \qquad V_j\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}\mathrm{Beta}(1,\kappa), \qquad \Theta_j\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}H_0.

In computation, the package uses a finite truncation level JJ (components) and works with fDPM(y∣𝐱)≈∑j=1Jwjk(y∣Θj,𝐱), f_{\mathrm{DPM}}(y\mid\boldsymbol{x})\;\approx\;\sum_{j=1}^{J} w_j\,k\!\left(y\mid\Theta_j,\boldsymbol{x}\right), where the final weight absorbs leftover mass. This is the standard blocked-Gibbs approximation for stick-breaking priors (Ishwaran and James 2001).

Define counts nj=∑i=1N𝕀(zi=j),mj=∑i=1N𝕀(zi>j). n_j = \sum_{i=1}^N\mathbb{I}(z_i=j),\qquad m_j = \sum_{i=1}^N\mathbb{I}(z_i>j). Then the stick variables have the conjugate full conditional Vj∣𝐳,κ∼Beta(1+nj,κ+mj), V_j\mid\boldsymbol{z},\kappa\;\sim\;\mathrm{Beta}(1+n_j,\;\kappa+m_j), and weights are reconstructed as above.

CRP backend (partition-based view)

DPmixGPD also offers a Chinese restaurant process backend (backend="crp"), which integrates out HH and updates allocations under the induced random partition distribution (Blackwell and MacQueen 1973; Neal 2000). In this view, observation ii joins an existing cluster with probability proportional to its current size, and starts a new cluster with probability proportional to κ\kappa, coupled with the base measure H0H_0.

Tail model: generalized Pareto distribution

Let u(𝐱)u(\boldsymbol{x}) be a (possibly covariate-dependent) threshold. For y>u(𝐱)y>u(\boldsymbol{x}), define exceedance e=y−u(𝐱)e=y-u(\boldsymbol{x}). The GPD model is Y∣Y>u(𝐱),𝐱∼GPD(u(𝐱),σ(𝐱),ξ), Y\mid Y>u(\boldsymbol{x}),\boldsymbol{x}\;\sim\;\mathrm{GPD}\big(u(\boldsymbol{x}),\sigma(\boldsymbol{x}),\xi\big), with scale σ(𝐱)>0\sigma(\boldsymbol{x})>0 and shape ξ∈ℝ\xi\in\mathbb{R}. The density is fGPD(y∣u(𝐱),σ(𝐱),ξ)={1σ(𝐱)(1+ξy−u(𝐱)σ(𝐱))−1/ξ−1,ξ≠0,1σ(𝐱)exp⁡(−y−u(𝐱)σ(𝐱)),ξ=0, f_{\mathrm{GPD}}\big(y\mid u(\boldsymbol{x}),\sigma(\boldsymbol{x}),\xi\big) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{\sigma(\boldsymbol{x})}\left(1+\xi\dfrac{y-u(\boldsymbol{x})}{\sigma(\boldsymbol{x})}\right)^{-1/\xi-1}, & \xi\neq 0,\\[10pt] \dfrac{1}{\sigma(\boldsymbol{x})}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y-u(\boldsymbol{x})}{\sigma(\boldsymbol{x})}\right), & \xi=0, \end{cases} with support constraint 1+ξ{y−u(𝐱)}/σ(𝐱)>01+\xi\{y-u(\boldsymbol{x})\}/\sigma(\boldsymbol{x})>0. The CDF is FGPD(y∣u(𝐱),σ(𝐱),ξ)={1−(1+ξy−u(𝐱)σ(𝐱))−1/ξ,ξ≠0,1−exp⁡(−y−u(𝐱)σ(𝐱)),ξ=0. F_{\mathrm{GPD}}\big(y\mid u(\boldsymbol{x}),\sigma(\boldsymbol{x}),\xi\big) = \begin{cases} 1-\left(1+\xi\dfrac{y-u(\boldsymbol{x})}{\sigma(\boldsymbol{x})}\right)^{-1/\xi}, & \xi\neq 0,\\[10pt] 1-\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y-u(\boldsymbol{x})}{\sigma(\boldsymbol{x})}\right), & \xi=0. \end{cases}

The use of the GPD for exceedances is justified by classical peaks-over-threshold limit theory (Balkema and Haan 1974; Pickands 1975; Coles 2001).

The spliced bulk–tail model

DPmixGPD defines a single proper density by splicing the bulk DPM and the GPD tail at u(𝐱)u(\boldsymbol{x}): f(y∣𝐱)={fDPM(y∣𝐱),y≤u(𝐱),(1−FDPM(u(𝐱)∣𝐱))fGPD(y∣u(𝐱),σ(𝐱),ξ),y>u(𝐱). f(y\mid\boldsymbol{x}) = \begin{cases} f_{\mathrm{DPM}}(y\mid\boldsymbol{x}), & y\le u(\boldsymbol{x}),\\[6pt] \Big(1-F_{\mathrm{DPM}}\big(u(\boldsymbol{x})\mid\boldsymbol{x}\big)\Big)\,f_{\mathrm{GPD}}\big(y\mid u(\boldsymbol{x}),\sigma(\boldsymbol{x}),\xi\big), & y>u(\boldsymbol{x}). \end{cases} The multiplicative factor 1−FDPM{u(𝐱)∣𝐱}1-F_{\mathrm{DPM}}\{u(\boldsymbol{x})\mid\boldsymbol{x}\} ensures that the total tail probability mass matches the bulk survival beyond the threshold.

Equivalently, the spliced CDF is F(y∣𝐱)={FDPM(y∣𝐱),y≤u(𝐱),FDPM(u(𝐱)∣𝐱)+(1−FDPM(u(𝐱)∣𝐱))FGPD(y∣u(𝐱),σ(𝐱),ξ),y>u(𝐱). F(y\mid\boldsymbol{x}) = \begin{cases} F_{\mathrm{DPM}}(y\mid\boldsymbol{x}), & y\le u(\boldsymbol{x}),\\[6pt] F_{\mathrm{DPM}}\big(u(\boldsymbol{x})\mid\boldsymbol{x}\big) +\Big(1-F_{\mathrm{DPM}}\big(u(\boldsymbol{x})\mid\boldsymbol{x}\big)\Big) \,F_{\mathrm{GPD}}\big(y\mid u(\boldsymbol{x}),\sigma(\boldsymbol{x}),\xi\big), & y>u(\boldsymbol{x}). \end{cases}

This section is the reference point for all later predictive quantities (density, survival, quantiles, exceedance probabilities).

Covariate dependence: general link mode

In conditional models, DPmixGPD allows (selected) parameters to depend on 𝐱\boldsymbol{x} via a link representation. For a generic parameter θj(𝐱)\theta_j(\boldsymbol{x}) in link mode, g(θj(𝐱))=ηj(𝐱)=𝐱⊤𝛃j, g\!\left(\theta_j(\boldsymbol{x})\right) = \eta_j(\boldsymbol{x}) = \boldsymbol{x}^\top\boldsymbol{\beta}_j, where g(⋅)g(\cdot) is a link function and 𝛃j\boldsymbol{\beta}_j is a component-specific regression coefficient. The package exposes this mechanism through param_specs, and it is not restricted to a single parameter: in principle, any parameter registered as linkable for a kernel can be placed in link mode.

The same idea applies to the tail module when a tail parameter is allowed to vary with 𝐱\boldsymbol{x} (for example, a covariate-dependent threshold or scale).

Likelihood and posterior computation

Likelihood

Under the spliced model, each observation contributes either a bulk likelihood term or a tail likelihood term, depending on whether it falls below or above the threshold. For a fixed threshold uu (no covariates), the full likelihood is ℒ(Θ,𝐰,σ,ξ∣𝐲)=∏i:yi≤ufDPM(yi)×∏i:yi>u(1−FDPM(u))fGPD(yi∣u,σ,ξ). \mathcal{L}(\Theta,\boldsymbol{w},\sigma,\xi\mid\boldsymbol{y}) = \prod_{i: y_i\le u} f_{\mathrm{DPM}}(y_i) \times \prod_{i: y_i>u} \Big(1-F_{\mathrm{DPM}}(u)\Big)\,f_{\mathrm{GPD}}(y_i\mid u,\sigma,\xi). With covariates, replace uu by u(𝐱i)u(\boldsymbol{x}_i) and use fDPM(⋅∣𝐱i)f_{\mathrm{DPM}}(\cdot\mid\boldsymbol{x}_i) and FDPM(⋅∣𝐱i)F_{\mathrm{DPM}}(\cdot\mid\boldsymbol{x}_i).

Posterior sampling (high-level algorithm)

DPmixGPD targets the joint posterior of mixture allocations, kernel parameters, weights, and (optionally) tail parameters.

For the stick-breaking backend, a typical blocked-Gibbs iteration updates:

  1. Allocation indicators ziz_i via categorical probabilities proportional to wjk(yi∣Θj,𝐱i)w_j\,k(y_i\mid\Theta_j,\boldsymbol{x}_i), with splicing handled by the tail module when yi>u(𝐱i)y_i>u(\boldsymbol{x}_i).
  2. Stick variables VjV_j (and hence wjw_j) from their conjugate beta full conditionals.
  3. Kernel parameters Θj\Theta_j from their full conditional distributions. When conjugacy is unavailable, the package uses Metropolis–Hastings steps inside the Gibbs loop.
  4. Tail parameters (u(⋅),σ(⋅),ξ)(u(\cdot),\sigma(\cdot),\xi) according to the user-specified param_specs. Fixed parameters are held constant; prior-based parameters are updated by conjugate or Metropolis steps depending on the chosen prior family.
  5. The concentration parameter κ\kappa if it is assigned a prior (typically a gamma prior), again via conjugate or Metropolis updates.

The CRP backend updates allocations under the partition distribution and then updates cluster parameters accordingly.

Posterior predictive quantities

Posterior prediction is implemented through the S3 method predict() for mixgpd_fit objects. For any functional g(θ)g(\theta) of the model parameters, prediction follows the standard Bayesian recipe: p(ỹ∣𝐱,𝒟)=∫p(ỹ∣𝐱,θ)p(θ∣𝒟)dθ, p(\tilde{y}\mid\boldsymbol{x},\mathcal{D}) = \int p(\tilde{y}\mid\boldsymbol{x},\theta)\,p(\theta\mid\mathcal{D})\,d\theta, approximated by Monte Carlo over posterior draws θ(s)\theta^{(s)}.

In particular,

  • density: f(ỹ∣𝐱,𝒟)≈S−1∑s=1Sf(ỹ∣𝐱,θ(s))f(\tilde{y}\mid\boldsymbol{x},\mathcal{D}) \approx S^{-1}\sum_{s=1}^S f(\tilde{y}\mid\boldsymbol{x},\theta^{(s)}),
  • survival: S(ỹ∣𝐱,𝒟)=1−F(ỹ∣𝐱,𝒟)S(\tilde{y}\mid\boldsymbol{x},\mathcal{D}) = 1-F(\tilde{y}\mid\boldsymbol{x},\mathcal{D}),
  • quantiles: Q(p∣𝐱,𝒟)Q(p\mid\boldsymbol{x},\mathcal{D}) obtained by inverting the posterior predictive CDF draw-by-draw and summarizing across draws.

Built-in example datasets

The source package ships several small datasets intended for examples and testing. Each dataset is an R object in data/ and can be loaded with data("<name>"). All of them are lists with a standard structure:

  • y: numeric outcome,
  • X: covariates (either NULL for unconditional or a data.frame for conditional),
  • T: treatment indicator (only for causal datasets),
  • meta: metadata (sample size, support, number of true components, whether a tail was injected),
  • truth: simulation truth (kernel family and parameters used to generate the dataset).

The workflow vignettes use these datasets to avoid generating new data.

List of 7
 $ n          : num 200
 $ support    : chr "positive"
 $ p          : int 0
 $ K_true     : num 4
 $ tail       : logi TRUE
 $ exceed_frac: num 0.12
 $ seed       : num 10103
List of 5
 $ kernel     : chr "lognormal"
 $ weights    : num [1:4] 0.3 0.25 0.25 0.2
 $ params     :List of 2
  ..$ meanlog: num [1:4] -0.2 0.2 0.6 1
  ..$ sdlog  : num [1:4] 0.4 0.35 0.45 0.5
 $ threshold  : num 3.04
 $ tail_params:List of 2
  ..$ scale: num 2.5
  ..$ shape: num 0.2
Balkema, August A., and Laurens de Haan. 1974. “Residual Life Time at Great Age.” The Annals of Probability 2 (5): 792–804. https://doi.org/10.1214/aop/1176996548.
Blackwell, David, and James B. MacQueen. 1973. “Ferguson Distributions via pólya Urn Schemes.” The Annals of Statistics 1 (2): 353–55. https://doi.org/10.1214/aos/1176342372.
Coles, Stuart. 2001. An Introduction to Statistical Modeling of Extreme Values. Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-4471-3675-0.
Ferguson, Thomas S. 1973. “A Bayesian Analysis of Some Nonparametric Problems.” The Annals of Statistics 1 (2): 209–30. https://doi.org/10.1214/aos/1176342360.
Ishwaran, Hemant, and Lancelot F. James. 2001. “Gibbs Sampling Methods for Stick-Breaking Priors.” Journal of the American Statistical Association 96 (453): 161–73. https://doi.org/10.1198/016214501750332758.
Neal, Radford M. 2000. “Markov Chain Sampling Methods for Dirichlet Process Mixture Models.” Journal of Computational and Graphical Statistics 9 (2): 249–65. https://doi.org/10.1080/10618600.2000.10474879.
Pickands, James. 1975. “Statistical Inference Using Extreme Order Statistics.” The Annals of Statistics 3 (1): 119–31. https://doi.org/10.1214/aos/1176343003.
Sethuraman, Jayaram. 1994. “A Constructive Definition of Dirichlet Priors.” Statistica Sinica 4 (2): 639–50.
